elements.sent-2411-001: With the advancement of large language models (LLMs), researchers have explored various methods to optimally leverage their comprehension and generation capabilities in sequential recommendation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, several challenges persist in this endeavor.
elements.sent-2411-003: Firstly, most existing approaches rely on the input-output prompting paradigm, which can result in irrelevant or inaccurate responses.
elements.sent-2411-004: Secondly, while there have been attempts to enhance LLMs using prompting strategies such as chain-of-thought (CoT), these efforts have not fully harnessed the reasoning abilities of LLMs or effectively captured the multifaceted information contained within user sequences.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address these limitations, we propose GOT4Rec, a sequential recommendation method that utilizes the graph of thoughts (GoT) prompting strategy.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, we identify and utilize three key types of information within user history sequences: short-term interests, long-term interests and collaborative information from other users.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our approach enables LLMs to independently reason and generate recommendations based on these distinct types of information, subsequently aggregating the results within the GoT framework to derive the final recommended items.
elements.sent-2411-008: This method allows LLMs, with enhanced reasoning capabilities, to more effectively consider the diverse information within user sequences, resulting in more accurate recommendations and more comprehensive explanations.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of GOT4Rec, indicating that it outperforms existing state-of-the-art baselines.
elements.sent-2411-011: Sequential recommendation has long been a significant research field, with numerous methods proposed to explore chronological dependencies within user sequences \cite{kang2018self,s3rec .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Despite considerable advancements, they remain constrained by the limited knowledge available from datasets.
elements.sent-2411-013: To overcome this, it is crucial to integrate real-world knowledge into sequential recommendation models, enabling them to more effectively comprehend and reason about preference patterns within user behavior sequences \cite{HouMZLDW22,LiWLFSSM23 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, large language models (LLMs) have garnered significant attention due to their impressive natural language comprehension and generation abilities.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, numerous LLM-based sequential recommendation works \cite{enhancer1,ranker4,ranker5 have emerged to exploit these capabilities, aiming to capture user interests from interaction sequences and leverage LLMs' extensive real-world knowledge for recommendations.}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, many of these approaches rely solely on the input-output (IO) prompting paradigm, underutilizing LLMs' reasoning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-017: This limitation arises from a disconnect between the real-world knowledge embedded in LLMs and the specific needs of recommender systems, often resulting in task-irrelevant or inaccurate outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-018: To address this, several studies have incorporated advanced prompting strategies to enhance LLMs' performance in sequential recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-019: Among these is SLIM \cite{wang2024can , a knowledge distillation module which employs chain-of-thought (CoT) \cite{wei2022chain prompting to enable step-by-step reasoning in sequential recommendation, transferring knowledge from a teacher model to a student model.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, it treats the user sequence as a monolithic entity and only taps into the elementary reasoning abilities of LLMs, insufficient for adequately reasoning through the diverse preference information (e.g., long-term and short-term interests, spatio-temporal factors) within user sequences.
elements.sent-2411-021: Overall, there are two major challenges that limit the effectiveness of LLMs in the sequential recommendation scenario.
elements.sent-2411-022: The first challenge is the difficulty in explicitly capturing various user preference information by merely prompting the behavior sequence.
elements.sent-2411-023: Traditional neural sequential recommenders have been actively extracting and fusing different types of information as features to predict the next item in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-024: In contrast, current LLM-based sequential recommenders often perform minimal processing of the sequence itself, simply placing the entire sequence directly into the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-025: This approach can easily mislead the model, leading to inaccurate recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-026: For instance, in Figure \ref{figure1 , the items predicted by SLIM consist solely of snack bars, while the ground truth is a fruit nut mix product.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The second challenge arises from the complexity introduced by incorporating multiple types of information, which transforms sequential recommendation into a complex reasoning task involving multiple sub-problems, necessitating LLMs with enhanced reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-028: Existing researches have demonstrated that simple reasoning approaches, such as IO and CoT, are insufficient for such tasks \cite{got,cotnotgood .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In contrast, the graph of thoughts (GoT) method offers a more effective solution by decomposing the problem into more manageable components.
elements.sent-2411-030: Unlike CoT, which employs a single chain of thought, GoT models the reasoning process through networked reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-031: This approach allows GoT to break down complex reasoning tasks into smaller, more tractable sub-tasks, solve them individually, and then integrate the results to form a comprehensive solution.
elements.sent-2411-032: In Figure \ref{figure1 , we illustrate how GoT is employed to reason about three categories of products (i.e., ``Probiotic Snack Bars", ``Healthy Snack Mixes" and ``Low-Sugar, Low-Carb Cookies") that the user might be interested in, enabling the LLM to independently recommend items within these categories and combine them in the end.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In contrast, SLIM's reasoning remains focused on snack bars.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/figure1.pdf} \caption{Comparison of the LLM output and predictions for the next item generated by SLIM \cite{wang2024can} and our proposed GOT4Rec.} \label{figure1}
elements.sent-2411-034: To address the challenges mentioned above, this paper proposes GOT4Rec, a novel method that introduces the graph of thoughts (GoT) framework into the field of sequential recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-035: GOT4Rec optimally harnesses the reasoning capabilities of LLMs while effectively integrating multiple sources of information from user sequences.
elements.sent-2411-036: Specifically, we employ the GoT prompting strategy to extract three critical sources of information from user sequences: short-term interests, long-term interests, and collaborative interests from other users with similar preferences.
elements.sent-2411-037: Unlike previous methods that treat the sequence as a whole,our approach considers multiple aspects of information and better utilizes the reasoning abilities of LLMs, leading to more accurate and interpretable recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our key contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-039: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to apply the graph of thoughts framework within the field of sequential recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-040: We introduce GOT4Rec, a method that optimally leverages the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) to comprehensively capture and utilize the rich information embedded within user sequences.
elements.sent-2411-041: Extensive experiments conducted on three datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms traditional neural sequential models and other prompting strategies.
elements.sent-2411-042: Traditional neural sequential recommendation systems aim to capture sequential dependencies in user behavior sequences to model dynamic user preferences, as seen in methods like GRU4Rec \cite{GRU4rec .}
elements.sent-2411-043: As deep learning develops, techniques like self-attention and graph neural networks have become foundational in sequential recommenders \cite{kang2018self, s3rec, wu2019session, graph1, graph2 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: These methods however, predominantly rely on sequence modeling capabilities and often inadequately incorporate textual information.
elements.sent-2411-045: Recently, research on transferable item representations \cite{HouMZLDW22,LiWLFSSM23 has gained attention.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Despite their promise, these approaches remain constrained by limited datasets and fail to fully leverage real-world knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-047: With the advent of LLMs, numerous research endeavors have explored leveraging their advanced language comprehension and generation abilities.
elements.sent-2411-048: LLMs can be integrated either as feature enhancers \cite{enhancer1,enhancer2,enhancer3 or rankers/scorers \cite{ranker1,ranker2,ranker3,ranker4,ranker5 .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: For example, ReLLa \cite{enhancer2 employs semantic user behavior retrieval to improve data quality and introduces retrieval-enhanced instruction tuning to enhance few-shot recommendation.}
elements.sent-2411-050: LLMRank \cite{ranker5 formalizes the recommendation problem as a conditional ranking task and utilizes LLMs as zero-short rankers.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Although these approaches are promising, existing research has not yet fully capitalized on the reasoning capabilities of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-052: Numerous prompting approaches have been proposed to exploit the reasoning capabilities of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-053: Chain-of-thought (CoT) \cite{wei2022chain introduces intermediate reasoning steps to enhance LLM performance, while Chain of Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC) \cite{cot-sc refines this by generating multiple CoTs and selecting the best output.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Tree of thoughts (ToT) \cite{yao2024tree and graph of thoughts (GoT) \cite{got further extend these methods by modeling reasoning as a tree or graph, respectively, to better generate and aggregate different thoughts.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: In the recommendation domain, SLIM \cite{wang2024can utilizes a CoT-based knowledge distillation module to transfer the step-by-step reasoning capabilities from a teacher model to a student model.}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, its reliance on basic CoT prompting limits its ability to fully capture the abundant information within user sequences.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/got4rec.pdf} \caption{An example of graph decomposition in the proposed GOT4Rec method. The user interaction sequence is divided to facilitate different types of reasoning: the last few items are used for short-term preference reasoning, while the entire sequence informs long-term preference reasoning. Additionally, sequences from similar users are retrieved for collaborative reasoning. In the thought graph, these distinct thoughts are generated and subsequently aggregated to capture and integrate the various aspects of information within the user interaction sequence.} \label{got4rec}
elements.sent-2411-057: In this section, we introduce GOT4Rec, a sequential recommendation method that fully leverages the reasoning capabilities of LLMs to capture the diverse information within user sequences.
elements.sent-2411-058: In the context of sequential recommendation, given a user $u$'s historical interaction sequence $S_{u = \{i_{1 , i_{2 , ...}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: When interacting with LLMs, we input messages (prompts) and LLMs respond with generated outputs (thoughts).
elements.sent-2411-060: Building on the GoT framework \cite{got , we model our GOT4Rec method as a tuple $(G, T)$, where $G$ represents the reasoning process and $T$ denotes the thought transformations.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Specifically, we model the reasoning process as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ is the set of vertices and $E\subseteq V\times V$ is the set of edges.
elements.sent-2411-062: Each vertex represents a thought, encapsulating a solution to the current recommendation step along with other relevant global information.
elements.sent-2411-063: A directed edge $(t_{1 , t_{2 )$ signifies a dependency between thoughts, indicating that thought $t_{2}$ is generated by LLMs based on $t_{1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: In our method, we employ the generation transformation $T_{G}$ and aggregation transformation $T_{A}$ from the GoT framework.
elements.sent-2411-065: The generation transformation generates one or more new thoughts based on an existing single thought $v$.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this operation, new vertices and edges are generated: $V_{+ = \{v_{1+ , ..., v_{k+}\}$ and $E_{+ = \{(v, v_{1+ ), ..., (v, v_{k+ )\}$, where $v_{1+ , ..., v_{k+}$ are the new thoughts generated by $v$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Analogous reasoning steps such as CoT can be incorporated into this process.
elements.sent-2411-068: The aggregation transformation, on the other hand, combines multiple thoughts into a new, consolidated thought, reinforcing their strengths while mitigating their weaknesses.
elements.sent-2411-069: In this operation, a new vertex $v_{+}$ is created: $V_{+ = \{v_{+}\}$ and $E_{+ = \{(v_{1 , v_{+ ), ..., (v_{k , v_{+ )\}$, where $v_{1 , ..., v_{k}$ are the aggregated thoughts.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Figure \ref{got4rec provides an example of graph decomposition in our GOT4Rec method.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In the recommendation pipeline, the current user's interaction sequence $S_{u}$ is utilized as input.
elements.sent-2411-072: We then generate three key aspects of user preference information: short-term preference, long-term preference and collaborative preference, with detailed definitions provided in the subsequent subsection.
elements.sent-2411-073: With these preference information, the LLMs are enabled to reason and generate a list of top-$N$ items that best align with the user's current interests.
elements.sent-2411-074: Finally, these items are aggregated, and the LLMs vote to select the most probable items for recommendation.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/prompt.pdf} \caption{Prompt templates, including summarizing, recommendation, collaboration and voting prompts.} \label{prompt}
elements.sent-2411-075: Numerous studies have emphasized the importance of understanding both a user's dynamic short-term and stable long-term preferences in recommendation systems \cite{short-long2,short-long3 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: In our GOT4Rec method, we enable LLMs to extract these two types of preferences separately and then synergize the most relevant aspects of each.
elements.sent-2411-077: To reason about short-term preferences, we select the last few interactions from the user sequence $S_{u}$ as $S_{short}$.
elements.sent-2411-078: We then apply and enhance the zero-shot CoT prompting strategy from \cite{wang2024can within the generation transformation $T_{G}$ to effectively capture the user's short-term preferences and identify three categories that the user is likely to favor.}
elements.sent-2411-079: As illustrated in Figure \ref{prompt as summarizing prompt, the generation transformation $T_{G}$ involves two key steps:.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Summarize user's short-term preferences based on the given $S_{short}$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Based on the preferences in step 1, summarize three categories of products the user is inclined to prefer.
elements.sent-2411-082: For each identified category, we prompt the LLMs to generate $N$ items the user is most likely interested in, repeating this process three times.
elements.sent-2411-083: The prompt template is provided in Figure \ref{prompt as recommendation prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-084: After generating the item sets, we utilize the aggregation transformation $T_{A}$, where the three item sets undergo a voting process by the LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-085: This results in a new set of $N$ items that are most likely to interest the user within each category.
elements.sent-2411-086: After aggergation, we have three final item sets, which are subjected to a final round of voting by the LLMs to determine the top-$N$ items $I_{short}$ that best represent the user's short-term preferences.
elements.sent-2411-087: The voting prompt is also provided in Figure \ref{prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-088: To capture long-term preferences, we extend the input to include the entire user interaction sequence.
elements.sent-2411-089: Similar to the short-term reasoning process, we employ the zero-short CoT prompting strategy within the generation transformation $T_{G}$, as shown in Figure \ref{prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-090: This allows LLMs to effectively differentiate and identify short-term and long-term preferences.
elements.sent-2411-091: After generating the relevant items, the final set of top-$N$ items $I_{long}$, representing the user's long-term preferences, is determined through the aggregation transformation $T_{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Collaborative filtering is widely used in various recommendation scenarios \cite{cf , modeling users’ preference based on their past interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-093: In our method, we leverage the all-mpnet-base-v2 \cite{all-mpnet-base-v2 , a sentence-transformers model that maps sentences to a vector space, to generate an embedding vector for the current user's interaction sequence, allowing us to retrieve a set of sequences $S_{co}$ from other users most similar to the current user's sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Details of this retrieval process are provided in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-095: Once we have obtained the similar user sequences $S_{co}$, we employ the zero-short CoT prompting strategy to generate three sets of top-$N$ items that the current user may be interested in.
elements.sent-2411-096: The two-step generation prompts, illustrated in Figure \ref{prompt as collaboration prompt, are as follows:.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Summarize the shared preferences between the current user and other users based on the given $S_{co}$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Based on the summarized preferences from Step 1, recommend $N$ items selected from $S_{co}$ that the current user is likely to prefer.
elements.sent-2411-099: The three sets of items generated through this process are then aggregated and selected by the LLMs using the aggregation transformation $T_{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-100: This results in the final set of top-$N$ items $I_{co}$ that best reflect the collaborative preferences of users with similar interests to the current user.
elements.sent-2411-101: At this point, we have obtained three distinct sets of items: $I_{short}$, which reflects the user's short-term preferences; $I_{long}$, which captures the user's long-term preferences; and $I_{co}$, which represents the collaborative preferences derived from other users.
elements.sent-2411-102: Finally, using the aggregation transformation $T_{A}$, we enable LLMs to synthesize these multiple sources of information to generate the final set of top-$N$ recommended items $I_{fin}$.
elements.sent-2411-103: The prompt template for this final aggregation is the same as the voting prompt.
elements.sent-2411-104: To identify other users' sequences that share same interests with the current user and to assess how closely the recommended items match the ground truth in sampled datasets, we employ a two-level retrieval approach.. Sequence-level retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-105: As previously mentioned, when analyzing collaborative preferences, it is essential to retrieve sequences of other users who share similar interests with the current user.
elements.sent-2411-106: We choose to deploy the all-mpnet-base-v2 model $f_{mpnet}$ due to its superior efficiency and discriminative power in encoding item titles compared to other encoding models such as BERT.
elements.sent-2411-107: Given a query sequence $S_{u = \{i_{1 , ..., i_{k}\}$, the ranked indices of the retrieved sequences $I_{seq}$ can be obtained as follows: \begin{equation}I_{seq} = sim(\frac{1}{k}\sum_{t=1}^{k}f_{mpnet}(i_{t}), V_{seq})\end{equation} where $sim(\cdot,\cdot)$ denotes the computation of Euclidean distance, $V_{seq}$ represents the vector base containing the embeddings of all other sequences.. Item-level retrieval.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The title of an item generated by LLMs may differ from the title of the ground truth in datasets, even though they refer to the same item.
elements.sent-2411-109: This discrepancy arises due to the limited and varied versions of items in the datasets (e.g., Witcher 3: Wild Hunt versus Witcher 3: Wild Hunt Complete Edition).
elements.sent-2411-110: To address this issue, we continue to utilize the $f_{mpnet}$ model to encode item titles into vectors.
elements.sent-2411-111: We then retrieve the most similar items from the vector base by computing the inner product of the query vector and all other vectors in the base.
elements.sent-2411-112: Specifically, for an item $i_{query}$ generated by LLMs, the retrieval process is as follows: \begin{equation}I_{item} = sim(f_{mpnet}(i_{query}), V_{item})\end{equation} where $I_{item}$ represents the ranked indices of the retrieved items, $sim(\cdot,\cdot)$ denotes the computation of the inner product and $V_{item}$ is the vector base containing all item embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-113: In practice, we observed that in most cases, when the description of the query item is vague, the retrieved items are misaligned (e.g., when querying for The Witcher 3, the top results include [Diablo III, The Witch and the Hundred Knight - PlayStation 3, The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt - Xbox One]).
elements.sent-2411-114: To mitigate this, we retrieve the top-$K$ items within the ranked indices for each recommended item.
elements.sent-2411-115: Overall, by empowering LLMs to independently reason and generate thoughts and then aggregate the most relevant results, our method fully leverages the reasoning capabilities of LLMs in the sequential recommendation scenario.
elements.sent-2411-116: This approach enables LLMs to effectively capture and integrate different aspects of the extensive information within user sequences.
elements.sent-2411-117: We conduct experiments on three item categories from the widely used Amazon Reviews'23 dataset \cite{hou2024bridginglanguageitemsretrieval : Video Games (Games), Grocery and Gourmet Food (Food) and Home and Kitchen (Home).}
elements.sent-2411-118: Reviews are treated as user-item interactions, sequenced chronologically by timestamp.
elements.sent-2411-119: To address the significant time cost of LLMs, we focus on users with 6 to 20 interactions and filter out items with fewer than five interactions.
elements.sent-2411-120: Following \cite{wang2024can , we randomly sample 3,000 users from each dataset three times.}
elements.sent-2411-121: We employ the leave-one-out strategy for dataset division: the most recent interaction for testing, the second most recent interaction for validation and the remaining interactions are used for training.
elements.sent-2411-122: Both training and validation segments are used as input during testing.
elements.sent-2411-123: To demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method, we select two groups of recommendation baselines.
elements.sent-2411-124: The first group comprises traditional neural sequential recommendation models:.
elements.sent-2411-125: GRU4Rec \cite{GRU4rec : Uses RNNs to model user sequences for session-based recommendation.}
elements.sent-2411-126: SRGNN \cite{wu2019session : A graph-based model for session-based recommendation to capture the transition information between items in user sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-127: SASRec \cite{kang2018self : A self-attention based sequential model to capture user’s preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-128: The second group contains models that utilize LLM prompting strategies:.
elements.sent-2411-129: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) \cite{wei2022chain : A prompting approach for prompting which includes the intermediate steps of reasoning within the prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Multiple CoTs (CoT-SC) \cite{cot-sc : A scheme in which multiple CoTs are generated, with the best one being selected as final result.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Tree of Thoughts (ToT) \cite{yao2024tree : A prompting approach modeling the LLM reasoning process as a tree.}
elements.sent-2411-132: SLIM \cite{wang2024can : A knowledge distillation module, which transfers the step-by-step reasoning capabilities in recommendation from a larger teacher model to a smaller student model.}
elements.sent-2411-133: We choose Llama3-8B-Instruct \cite{llama3 as the backbone model with a maximum token length of 4096.}
elements.sent-2411-134: The LMDeploy toolkit \cite{lmdeploy is employed to deploy LLMs and accelerate reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-135: GRU4Rec, SASRec and SRGNN are implemented based on their official code, and LLM prompting strategies are integrated within the GoT framework.
elements.sent-2411-136: For SLIM, Llama3-8B-Instruct is used instead of ChatGPT to avoid high API costs.
elements.sent-2411-137: We retrieve the 10 most similar items for both GOT4Rec and baseline methods using the faiss library \cite{faiss .}
elements.sent-2411-138: Optimal hyper-parameters for all baselines are carefully selected to ensure the best performance.. Evaluation Metrics.
elements.sent-2411-139: We evaluate performance using hit rate (HR) and normalized discounted cumulative gain (NDCG), reporting HR@K and NDCG@K for $K\in\{5, 10, 20\}$.
elements.sent-2411-140: Each recommended item is evaluated against all other items in the sampled datasets.
elements.sent-2411-141: To assess recommendation novelty, we calculate EFD@10 and EPC@10 \cite{VargasC11 .}
elements.sent-2411-142: The average scores of three runs are reported.
elements.sent-2411-143: Table \ref{results compares our GOT4Rec method with various neural sequential models and LLM prompting strategies, leading to several key observations: (1) Traditional neural sequential models perform relatively modest, though SASRec still outperforms LLM prompting strategies in some cases, particularly due to its use of self-attention mechanisms which allow SASRec to effectively model users' historical behaviors by capturing the transition relationships between items.}
elements.sent-2411-144: However, SASRec lacks the ability to comprehend semantic information, limiting its overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-145: On the other hand, CoT, ToT, and SLIM show comparatively lower performance.
elements.sent-2411-146: SLIM, in particular, may suffer from reduced output diversity due to fine-tuning, while ToT's structural and reasoning path designs appear to be less suitable for sequential recommendation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-147: Notably, in the Food dataset, GOT4Rec achieves a relative improvement of 73.93\% over CoT-SC in terms of NDCG@10 and 67.49\% in terms of HR@5.
elements.sent-2411-148: These significant gains can be attributed to the nature of food product consumption, where users prefer consistent categories or brands and are strongly influenced by short-term needs.
elements.sent-2411-149: This aligns well with the strengths of GOT4Rec, which excels at capturing users' preferences for certain categories or brands and effectively integrating short-term preference information.
elements.sent-2411-150: This capability allows GOT4Rec to deliver highly relevant recommendations that closely match users' current interests.
elements.sent-2411-151: These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of GOT4Rec in optimizing recommendation tasks by fully exploiting the advanced reasoning capabilities of LLMs and integrating various aspects of user information.
elements.sent-2411-152: We conducted an ablation study to analyze the impact of different components in the GOT4Rec model, with the results in Table \ref{tab:ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-153: Our GOT4Rec consistently outperforms the ablated variants, demonstrating that the full integration of users' preference information from the short-term, long-term, and collaborative components results in superior recommendation performance.
elements.sent-2411-154: The study also reveals that the importance of each component varies across different datasets, likely reflecting the unique characteristics of each dataset.
elements.sent-2411-155: For instance, collaborative information appears to be the most crucial component in Games dataset.
elements.sent-2411-156: This suggests that when purchasing gaming products, users prioritize categories or brands, making collaborative information from other users with similar interests particularly important.
elements.sent-2411-157: In contrast, short-term preference plays a more significant role in Food dataset, aligning with our inference that users' recent preferences heavily influence their choices in food products.
elements.sent-2411-158: In Home dataset, the impact of each component varies, but short-term preference has the least influence.
elements.sent-2411-159: This could indicate that long-term preferences or collaborative insights are more critical when users make decisions about home-related items.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/game_bias.pdf} \caption{Analysis of popularity bias in Games dataset. Items in the dataset are sorted by their frequency. Compared to CoT, GOT4Rec demonstrates a more consistent ability to recommend long-tail items.} \label{game_bias}
elements.sent-2411-160: In Figure \ref{game_bias , we sort the items in Games dataset based on their frequency in the training set (i.e., popularity) and draw lines to illustrate each item's frequency in the results of CoT and GOT4Rec.}
elements.sent-2411-161: The figures for other two datasets are presented in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-162: It is apparent that GOT4Rec more effectively recommends tail items and a broader variety of items.
elements.sent-2411-163: Table \ref{tab:bias reports EFD@10 and EPC@10 metrics for CoT, SLIM and GOT4Rec, indicating tthat GOT4Rec outperforms the baselines in recommending long-tail items.}
elements.sent-2411-164: These results support our claim that GOT4Rec mitigates popularity bias by capturing a wider range of information.
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{tabular}{ccccc} Dataset Metric CoT SLIM GOT4Rec \multirow{2}{*}{Games} EFD@10 4.8919 4.1292 5.0929 EPC@10 0.3885 0.3306 0.3998 \multirow{2}{*}{Food} EFD@10 6.4721 5.3517 6.8671 EPC@10 0.4750 0.3923 0.5035 \multirow{2}{*}{Home} EFD@10 4.1911 3.1141 4.6019 EPC@10 0.3110 0.2304 0.3450 \end{tabular} \caption{Popularity bias metrics, the higher score indicates higher recommendation novelty. The best performance is highlighted in bold.} \label{tab:bias}
elements.sent-2411-165: In this paper, we propose GOT4Rec, a sequential recommendation method that optimally leverages the reasoning capabilities of LLMs to extract and integrate short-term, long-term, and collaborative user preferences utilizing the graph of thoughts (GoT) framework.
elements.sent-2411-166: Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our GOT4Rec method outperforms existing neural sequential models and LLM prompting strategies.
elements.sent-2411-167: Further analysis reveals that GOT4Rec effectively integrates multiple pieces of information contained within user sequences for superior recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-168: With their vast open-world knowledge and reasoning abilities, large language models (LLMs) have become a promising tool for sequential recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-169: Researchers have explored various methods to harness these capabilities, but most existing approaches rely on simple input-output prompting, failing to effectively bridge the gap between LLMs' general knowledge and the specific needs of recommendation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-170: While reasoning strategies like chain-of-thought (CoT) have been introduced to enhance performance, they often produce inaccurate recommendations due to underutilized user preference information and insufficient reasoning depth.
elements.sent-2411-171: To address these challenges, we propose GOT4Rec, a novel sequential recommendation method leveraging the graph of thoughts (GoT) reasoning strategy.
elements.sent-2411-172: Our method focuses on three key types of information in user histories: short-term interests, long-term interests and collaborative information from other users.
elements.sent-2411-173: It enables LLMs to reason independently and generate recommendations, subsequently aggregating results to derive final items.
elements.sent-2411-174: This method allows LLMs, with enhanced reasoning capabilities, to better utilize the user sequence information, producing more accurate recommendations and comprehensive explanations.
elements.sent-2411-175: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of GOT4Rec, outperforming existing state-of-the-art baselines with an average improvement of 37.11\%.
elements.sent-2411-177: Large language models (LLMs) have recently attracted significant attention for their expansive open-world knowledge and advanced reasoning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-178: Consequently, numerous LLM-based sequential recommendation methods \cite{enhancer1,ranker4,ranker5 have emerged, aiming to capture user interests from interaction sequences while leveraging LLMs’ comprehensive real-world knowledge for recommendations.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Many of these approaches rely solely on the input-output prompting paradigm, which underutilizes the reasoning potential of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-180: As a result, it often produces task-irrelevant or inaccurate outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-181: To address this problem, several studies have explored advanced reasoning strategies to boost LLMs’ performance in sequential recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-182: One example is SLIM \cite{wang2024can , a knowledge distillation module which employs chain-of-thought (CoT) \cite{wei2022chain prompting to enable step-by-step reasoning in sequential recommendation, transferring knowledge from a teacher model to a student model.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: However, it only utilizes the basic reasoning abilities of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-184: This is insufficient for effectively processing the rich preference information embedded in user sequences, such as long-term and short-term interests or spatio-temporal patterns.
elements.sent-2411-185: Consequently, it often produces inaccurate recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-186: As illustrated in Figure \ref{figure1 , SLIM’s CoT-based reasoning focuses narrowly on predicting snack bars, while the true preference is a fruit nut mix, highlighting its limitations in handling diverse preference structures.}
elements.sent-2411-187: Therefore, the challenge lies in integrating diverse user preferences into a cohesive reasoning framework, turning sequential recommendation into a multi-faceted task requiring advanced reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-188: Studies have shown that simple approaches like CoT are inadequate for tasks that demand decomposition into sub-tasks \cite{got,cotnotgood .}
elements.sent-2411-189: This approach allows GoT to break down the complex user preference reasoning tasks into smaller, more tractable sub-tasks, solve them individually, and then integrate the results to form a comprehensive solution.
elements.sent-2411-190: As illustrated in Figure \ref{figure1 , GoT reasons over multiple product categories that the user might be interested in: Probiotic Snack Bars, Healthy Snack Mixes and Low-Sugar, Low-Carb Cookies, enabling the LLM to independently generate recommendations within each category and combine them to correctly predict the ground truth item: fruit nut mix.}
elements.sent-2411-191: While SLIM's recommendation remains focused on snack bars, showcasing its narrower approach.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/figure1.pdf} \caption{Comparison of the LLM output and predictions for the next item generated by SLIM and our proposed GOT4Rec.} \label{figure1}
elements.sent-2411-192: Specifically, we employ the GoT reasoning strategy to extract three critical sources of information from user sequences: short-term interests, long-term interests, and collaborative interests from other users with similar preferences.
elements.sent-2411-193: To the best of our knowledge, this work is among the first to explore the application of the graph of thoughts framework in the context of sequential recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-194: We introduce GOT4Rec, which optimally leverages the reasoning capabilities of LLMs to comprehensively capture and utilize the information within user sequences.
elements.sent-2411-195: Extensive experiments conducted on three datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms neural and LLM-based sequential models as well as other LLM reasoning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-196: Techniques like self-attention and graph neural networks have further advanced the field \cite{kang2018self,s3rec,wu2019session,graph1,graph2 .}
elements.sent-2411-197: These approaches remain constrained by limited datasets and fail to fully leverage real-world knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-198: With the advent of LLMs, new potential are offered by leveraging LLMs' language understanding for recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-199: They can enhance features \cite{enhancer1,enhancer2,enhancer3 or act as rankers \cite{ranker1,ranker2,ranker3,ranker4,ranker5 .}}
elements.sent-2411-200: Numerous approaches have been proposed to exploit the reasoning capabilities of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-201: Tree of thoughts (ToT) \cite{yao2024tree and graph of thoughts (GoT) \cite{got further extend these methods by modeling reasoning as a tree or graph, respectively, to better generate and aggregate different thoughts, thereby improving complex tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: However, its reliance on basic CoT limits its ability to fully capture the abundant sequential dependencies and intricate patterns within user interactions.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figure/got4rec.pdf} \caption{An example of graph decomposition in the proposed GOT4Rec method. The user interaction sequence is divided to facilitate different types of reasoning: the last few items are used for short-term preference reasoning, while the entire sequence informs long-term preference reasoning. Additionally, sequences from similar users are retrieved for collaborative reasoning. In the thought graph, these thoughts are generated and subsequently aggregated to capture and integrate the various aspects of information in the user interaction sequence.} \label{got4rec}
elements.sent-2411-203: In our method, we employ the generation transformation $T_{G}$ and aggregation transformation $T_{A}$ from GoT framework.
elements.sent-2411-204: The generation transformation generates one or more new thoughts based on an existing thought $v$.
elements.sent-2411-205: In this operation, new vertices and edges are generated: $V_{+ = \{v_{1+ , ..., v_{k+}\}$ and $E_{+ = \{(v, v_{1+ ), ..., (v, v_{k+ )\}$, where $v_{1+ , ..., v_{k+}$ are new thoughts generated by $v$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Reasoning steps such as CoT can be incorporated into this process.
elements.sent-2411-207: While the aggregation transformation combines multiple thoughts into a consolidated thought, reinforcing their strengths while mitigating their weaknesses.
elements.sent-2411-208: A new vertex $v_{+}$ is created in this operation: $V_{+ = \{v_{+}\}$ and $E_{+ = \{(v_{1 , v_{+ ), ..., (v_{k , v_{+ )\}$, where $v_{1 , ..., v_{k}$ are the aggregated thoughts.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-209: To reason about short-term preferences, we select the last few interactions from the user sequence $S_{u}$ as $S_\text{short}$.
elements.sent-2411-210: We then apply and enhance the zero-shot CoT strategy from \cite{wang2024can within the generation transformation $T_{G}$ to effectively capture the user's short-term preferences and identify three categories that the user is likely to favor.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Summarize user's short-term preferences based on the given $S_\text{short}$.
elements.sent-2411-212: After aggergation, we have three final item sets, which are subjected to a final round of voting by the LLMs to determine the top-$N$ items $I_\text{short}$ that best represent the user's short-term preferences.
elements.sent-2411-213: Similar to the short-term reasoning process, we employ the zero-short CoT strategy within the generation transformation $T_{G}$, as shown in Figure \ref{prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-214: After generating the relevant items, the final set of top-$N$ items $I_\text{long}$, representing the user's long-term preferences, is determined through the aggregation transformation $T_{A}$.
elements.sent-2411-215: Collaborative filtering is widely used in various recommendation scenarios, modeling users’ preference based on their past interactions.
elements.sent-2411-216: Once we have obtained the similar user sequences $S_\text{co}$, we employ the zero-short CoT strategy to generate three sets of top-$N$ items that the current user may be interested in.
elements.sent-2411-217: Summarize the shared preferences between the current user and other users based on the given $S_\text{co}$.
elements.sent-2411-218: Based on the summarized preferences from Step 1, recommend $N$ items selected from $S_\text{co}$ that the current user is likely to prefer.
elements.sent-2411-219: This results in the final set of top-$N$ items $I_\text{co}$ that best reflect the collaborative preferences of users with similar interests to the current user.
elements.sent-2411-220: At this point, we have obtained three distinct sets of items: $I_\text{short}$, which reflects the user's short-term preferences; $I_\text{long}$, which captures the user's long-term preferences; and $I_\text{co}$, which represents the collaborative preferences derived from other users.
elements.sent-2411-221: Finally, using the aggregation transformation $T_{A}$, we enable LLMs to synthesize these multiple sources of information to generate the final set of top-$N$ recommended items $I_\text{fin}$.
elements.sent-2411-222: To identify other users' sequences that share same interests with the current user and to assess how closely the recommended items match the ground truth in sampled datasets, we employ a two-level retrieval approach.
elements.sent-2411-223: We choose to deploy the Mpnet \cite{all-mpnet-base-v2 model $f_\text{mpnet}$ due to its superior efficiency and discriminative power in encoding item titles compared to other encoding models such as BERT.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Given a query sequence $S_{u = \{i_{1 , ..., i_{m}\}$, the retrieved sequences set ${\mathcal{S _{\mathcal{V _\text{seq ^{k }$ can be obtained as follows: \begin{equation}{\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{V}_\text{seq}}^{k}}(i_t) \triangleq {\arg\max_{\boldsymbol{v} \in \mathcal{V}_\text{seq}}^k} sim(\frac{1}{m}\sum_{t=1}^{m}f_\text{mpnet}(i_{t}), \boldsymbol{v})\end{equation} where ${\mathcal{S _{\mathcal{V _\text{seq ^{k }$ is the set of top-$k$ sequences that share similar interests with the current user.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Specifically, for an item $i_\text{query}$ generated by LLMs, the retrieval process is as follows: \begin{equation}{\mathcal{I}_{\mathcal{V}_\text{item}}^{k}}(i_\text{query}) \triangleq {\arg\max_{\boldsymbol{v} \in \mathcal{V}_\text{item}}^k} sim(f_\text{mpnet}(i_\text{query}), \boldsymbol{v})\end{equation} where ${\mathcal{I _{\mathcal{V _\text{item ^{k }$ represents the set of the retrieved top-$k$ items based on $sim(\cdot,\cdot)$, which denotes the computation of the inner product and $V_\text{item}$ is the vector base containing all item embeddings.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-226: In Table \ref{tab:time , we compare the inference lantency and volume between reasoning frameworks.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Lantency is the number of hops to reach the final thought and volume refer to the number of preceding thoughts that have impacted the final thought \cite{got .}
elements.sent-2411-228: The time to generate a single thought is assumed to be $O(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-229: In CoT, reasoning follows a single sequential chain, resulting in both latency and volume scaling with $N$.
elements.sent-2411-230: CoT-SC employs $k$ independent chains, noting that these chains support parallel execution, both are reduced to $N/k$.
elements.sent-2411-231: For a $k$-ray ToT tree, both latency and volume are $\log_{k N$.}
elements.sent-2411-232: As for GoT, it is a $k$-ray tree combined with its inverted counterpart, resulting in a latency of $\log_{k N$.}
elements.sent-2411-233: For volume, since all intermediate thoughts contribute to the final thought by aggregating information from every step, it scales with $N$.
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{tabular}{lll} Framework Latency Volume Chain-of-Thought (CoT) $N$ $N$ Multiple CoTs (CoT-SC) $N/k$ $N/k$ Tree of Thoughts (ToT) $\log_{k}N$ $\log_{k}N$ Graph of Thoughts (GoT) $\log_{k}N$ $N$ \end{tabular} \caption{Comparison of inference latency and volume for different reasoning frameworks.} \label{tab:time}
elements.sent-2411-234: We conduct experiments on three item categories from Amazon Reviews'23 dataset \cite{hou2024bridginglanguageitemsretrieval : Video Games (Games), Grocery and Gourmet Food (Food) and Home and Kitchen (Home).}
elements.sent-2411-235: Reviews are treated as user-item interactions, sequenced chronologically by timestamps.
elements.sent-2411-236: We focus on users with 6 to 20 interactions and filter out items with fewer than five interactions.
elements.sent-2411-237: We employ the leave-one-out strategy for dataset division: the most recent interaction for testing, the second most recent interaction for validation and the remaining are used for training.
elements.sent-2411-238: The first group comprises both neural and LLM-based sequential recommenders:.
elements.sent-2411-239: PALR \cite{palr : A novel framework that integrates user behavior with LLMs to generate personalized recommendations.}
elements.sent-2411-240: TALLRec \cite{tallrec : An efficient fine-tuning framework that aligns LLMs with recommendation systems through a two-stage tuning process.}
elements.sent-2411-241: The second group contains models that utilize LLM reasoning strategies:.
elements.sent-2411-242: SLIM \cite{wang2024can : A knowledge distillation module transferring the step-by-step reasoning capabilities in recommendation from a larger teacher model to a smaller student model.}
elements.sent-2411-243: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) \cite{wei2022chain : A reasoning approach for prompting which includes the intermediate steps of reasoning within the prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Tree of Thoughts (ToT) \cite{yao2024tree : A reasoning approach modeling LLM reasoning process as a tree.}
elements.sent-2411-245: We choose Llama3-8B-Instruct \cite{llama3 as the backbone model.}
elements.sent-2411-246: SASRec and TALLRec are implemented based on their official code.
elements.sent-2411-247: We implemented PALR by ourselves and LLM reasoning strategies are integrated within the GoT framework.
elements.sent-2411-248: Optimal hyper-parameters for all baselines are carefully selected to ensure the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-249: We evaluate performance with hit rate (HR) and normalized discounted cumulative gain (NDCG), reporting HR@K and NDCG@K for $K\in\{5, 10, 20\}$.
elements.sent-2411-250: Each recommended item is evaluated against all other items in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-251: Table \ref{results compares our GOT4Rec method with various neural sequential models and LLM reasoning strategies, leading to several key observations:.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Neural and LLM-based sequential models perform relatively modest, though SASRec still outperforms LLM reasoning strategies in some cases, particularly due to its use of self-attention mechanisms which allow SASRec to effectively model users' historical behaviors by capturing the transition relationships between items.
elements.sent-2411-253: As for PALR and TALLRec, their suboptimal performance stems from the absence of strong reasoning capabilities, which are critical for complex recommendation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-254: Among LLM reasoning methods, CoT-SC consistently achieves runner-up performance across most datasets, largely because it aggregates and selects the best result from multiple CoT paths, providing a more refined output.
elements.sent-2411-255: GOT4Rec achieves the state-of-art performances across all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-256: This capability allows GOT4Rec to deliver highly relevant recommendations that closely match users' interests.

The performance difference (\%) with GOT4Rec is highlighted with {\color{RoyalBlue}{blue}} and {\color{Salmon}{orange}}. ``Short-term", ``Long-term", and ``Collaborative" denote short-term, long-term and collaborative reasoning steps, respectively.} \label{tab:ablation}
elements.sent-2411-257: In contrast, short-term preference plays a more significant role in the Food dataset, as users’ recent interactions are often influenced by immediate needs, cravings, or specific dietary goals.
elements.sent-2411-258: This aligns with our inference that users' recent preferences heavily influence their choices in food products.
elements.sent-2411-259: In the Home dataset, the impact of each component varies, but short-term preference has the least influence.
elements.sent-2411-260: This indicates that long-term preferences or collaborative insights are more critical when users make decisions about home-related items that typically involve more thoughtful consideration.
elements.sent-2411-261: EFD (Expected Free Discovery) represents the expected inverse collection frequency of relevant and seen recommended items.
elements.sent-2411-262: EPC (Expected Popularity Complement) measures the expected number of seen relevant recommended items that were previously unknown to the user.
elements.sent-2411-263: These two metrics provide a comprehensive assessment of diversity and novelty, helping to balance the recommendation of popular items with long-tail content.
elements.sent-2411-264: Table \ref{tab:bias reports EFD@10 and EPC@10 metrics for CoT, SLIM and GOT4Rec, indicating that GOT4Rec outperforms the baselines in recommending long-tail items.}

elements.sent-2411-265: Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our GOT4Rec method outperforms existing neural sequential models and LLM reasoning strategies.
elements.sent-2411-266: Further analysis reveals that GOT4Rec effectively integrates multiple pieces of information contained within user sequences for superior recommendations.. \bibliographystyle{named .}

elements.sent-2411-272: It is evident from the figure that GOT4Rec demonstrates a significant advantage in recommending tail items.
elements.sent-2411-273: Additionally, GOT4Rec achieves a broader coverage of items, indicating its ability to better capture and utilize the long-tail distribution.

elements.sent-2411-274: To evaluate the generalizability of our method, we conduct experiments using a variety of alternative LLM backbones.
elements.sent-2411-275: Specifically, we test Llama3.2-3B-Instruct \cite{llama3-2 , Gemma2-9B-Instruct \cite{gemma and Command-R-7B \cite{commandr , which represent a diverse set of model architectures and parameter scales.}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: These backbones were selected to provide a robust and comprehensive evaluation of our method.
elements.sent-2411-277: The results, presented in Table \ref{tab:generalization , demonstrate that our method remains effective on the Food dataset, but exhibits uneven performance on the other two datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-278: In both the Food and Home datasets, the HRs are generally above the baselines, but the NDCGs are noticeably lower, indicating that while relevant items are being recommended, their ranking positions are suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-279: We speculate that these discrepancies stem from inherent differences in the LLMs themselves, as their knowledge bases and reasoning abilities vary significantly, which can impact their ability to process and prioritize diverse types of information.
